title: On Being Confident
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: On Being Confident - 1 John 5_14-17 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.point: I. Introduction: God Wants His People to Be Humbly Confident
subpoints: A. John's purpose: that believers may know they believe (1 John 5:13)
B. Four sources of confidence in 1 John
A. We will be received by Christ at his coming (1 John 2:28)
B. Our sins have been forgiven and cannot be held against us (1 John 3:19-21)
C. We will be acquitted in the last judgment (1 John 4:17-19)

outline.point: III. The Fourth Confidence: Access to God in Prayer
subpoints: A. Christ has opened the doors of the Most Holy Place for us
B. We can come to God at any moment and he will listen
C. The one limitation: praying according to his revealed will

outline.point: IV. God's Hearing and God's Answering
subpoints: A. God hears and answers every prayer of his people (v. 15)
B. His answer may differ from our words—but it is always wisest
C. Even the dreaded no, the dreaded death, is a glorious answer

outline.point: V. The Exception: Sin That Leads to Death
subpoints: A. Two views: physical death (Ananias and Sapphira) or apostasy
B. Apostasy: willful, persistent rejection of Christ after knowing him (Hebrews 6:4-6)
C. Application: cling to Christ; do not let him go

outline.point: VI. Conclusion: Pray for One Another
subpoints: A. John's positive point: use your access to God to pray for struggling brothers
B. The church as a community that shares struggles and prays together
C. Be confident, come boldly before your Father in heaven

summary:
Preaching through 1 John 5:14-17, Pastor Tito Lyro expounds the fourth and final confidence John gives believers: that Christ has opened the Most Holy Place and believers can come before God in prayer at any moment, knowing he hears them. He reviews the three previous confidences in the letter—being received at Christ's coming, having sins forgiven, being acquitted at the last judgment—then focuses on the remarkable truth that God hears every prayer offered according to his revealed will and answers according to his infinite wisdom, even when the answer is 'no.'

The sermon addresses the perplexing passage about sin that leads to death, concluding that it most likely refers to persistent, informed apostasy—willfully rejecting Christ after knowing him—rather than merely mortal sin. The practical application is a double exhortation: first, cling to Christ and do not let him go; second, become a church that humbly shares struggles and intercedes for one another, taking full advantage of the access to the Father that Christ purchased.
transcript:
## Introduction: God Wants His People to Be Humbly Confident

God wants his people to be humbly confident in the face of what Jesus Christ has done for them. God wants us to be confident in Christ. If you are trusting in Jesus Christ, God wants you to be confident in what Jesus has done for you.

John is writing this epistle to the church in Ephesus, to encourage them to stand in their faith against false teachers who denied who Christ is. For John, the Christian — the one who believes in the whole Christ of the Bible, the one who believes that the life Christ lived he lived for us, that the death he died was in our place, and that Christ rose from the dead so that we could have life — that one should have confidence in his faith.

We see that in verse 13, just above our passage: John is writing this letter so that believers may *know* that they believe in the Lord Jesus Christ. He wrote the Gospel so that you may believe. Now this epistle is a follow-up on the Gospel, saying, I am writing to you so that you know what true faith is — so that you can examine your faith and know that you believe in the Lord Jesus Christ.

Four times in this short letter, John states that the believer is confident in Christ — confident in the gospel, confident in his Savior. This morning we will briefly review the first three reasons for confidence in Christ, which we have looked at in previous passages, and then spend a little more time on the fourth reason for confidence found in verses 14 through 17 of chapter 5.

## I. The First Confidence: We Will Be Received by Christ at His Coming (1 John 2:28)

The first reason John teaches us that we have confidence in Christ is that we will be received by Christ at his coming, if we abide in him. In chapter 2 verse 28, the apostle says:

> *"And now, little children, abide in him, so that when he appears we may have confidence and not shrink from him in shame at his coming."* (1 John 2:28)

Those who believe in the Lord Jesus Christ — those who abide in him, those who believe in him now — will stand before him at his return.

To abide is to remain, to dwell, to be rooted and grounded in Jesus Christ. Old Testament prophets often ask: who shall stand at the day of the coming of the Lord? Malachi in chapter 3 verse 2 asks, "Who can endure the day of his coming, and who can stand when he appears?" Joel in chapter 2 verse 11 says, "The Lord utters his voice before his army, for his camp is exceedingly great; he who executes his word is powerful. For the day of the Lord is great and very awesome; who can endure it?"

You and I as believers in Jesus Christ have a simple answer: those who have trusted in the Lord Jesus Christ can endure the day of the Lord. If you are abiding in Jesus Christ, you look forward to the coming of the Lord, because those who trust in the Lord will hear from the mouth of their Savior at his coming: "Come, you who are blessed by my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world."

## II. The Second Confidence: Our Sins Have Been Forgiven (1 John 3:19–21)

The second confidence, in chapter 3 verses 19 through 21: we can be confident that our sins have been forgiven. John says in verse 19:

> *"By this we shall know that we are of the truth and reassure our heart before him; for whenever our heart condemns us, God is greater than our heart, and he knows everything. Beloved, if our heart does not condemn us, we have confidence before God."* (1 John 3:19–21)

If you are a true believer, not even your heart can condemn you, because Christ has declared you not guilty. Your sins have been forgiven. If you believe in the Lord Jesus Christ as taught in the Scriptures, you can be confident that all your sins have been forgiven. Chapter 1 verse 9 says that Christ is faithful and just to forgive our sins. God does not hold your sins against you. They have all been declared to be Christ's, and he paid for them on the cross.

> *"For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God."* (2 Corinthians 5:21)

> *"As far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us."* (Psalm 103:12)

> *"No longer shall each one teach his neighbor and each his brother, saying, 'Know the Lord,' for they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest, declares the Lord. For I will forgive their iniquity, and I will remember their sin no more."* (Jeremiah 31:34)

That is true of you, believer. Nothing can separate you from the love of God in Jesus Christ. Who can bring a charge against you? It is Christ who justifies. Who can accuse you before the Father? It is Christ who stands before you and intercedes for your soul.

Because all your sins have been forgiven, there is nothing that can separate you from your Father in heaven. God loves you with the same love with which he loves his own Son. You know the darkness of your heart. You know the things you have done against God. And yet when God looks at you, he loves you with the same infinite love with which he loves his Son. And that never changes. The love of God for you does not increase and does not decrease. You cannot earn the love of God — it is already yours. It is infinite. It does not change even when you have a really bad day. It does not change even when you fight with your spouse. You know what we sometimes call preparation for worship: fighting in the minivan all the way to church. Even in those times, the love of God for you does not change. It does not change when you fail as a parent. It does not change when you do not read your Bible and do not pray. It does not change even in those times when you are unfaithful to him.

Brothers and sisters, if your faith is real, you can be confident that your sins are forgiven.

Remember the paralytic who was brought to Jesus by his friends. The room was crowded — they could not get through — so they went up on the roof, removed the tiles, and lowered the paralytic down into the room. Remember what Jesus says? "Your sins are forgiven." And then he says: which is easier, to forgive sins or to make him walk? Get up and walk — and the man gets up and walks. But more important than the healing, Jesus says: your sins are forgiven.

Church of Jesus Christ: Jesus says that to you today. Your sins are forgiven. You can be confident of that. They do not stand between you and your God.

## III. The Third Confidence: We Will Be Acquitted in the Last Judgment (1 John 4:17–19)

The third confidence, in chapter 4 verses 17 through 19: the believer will be acquitted in the last judgment.

> *"By this is love perfected with us, so that we may have confidence for the day of judgment, because as he is so also are we in this world. There is no fear in love, but perfect love casts out fear. For fear has to do with punishment, and whoever fears has not been perfected in love."* (1 John 4:17–18)

God will pronounce the final verdict on us at the coming of the Lord Jesus Christ. Earthly courts can only declare you not guilty — they cannot declare you innocent. Those are different things. But Christ, who is the ultimate judge, will declare all his people innocent. That will be a final declaration of a current reality. You are already innocent in the sight of God. Those who believe in the Lord Jesus Christ stand innocent of their sin — and not just innocent of sin, but positively righteous, because when the Father looks at you, he sees his Son. He looks at your face and sees Jesus Christ, who was made our righteousness.

Because of that, we do not have to fear the final judgment. That is what John means when he says that love casts out fear. We can pray, "Come quickly, Lord Jesus." Would it not be wonderful if Christ came back today? All suffering done. All sinning done. All that causes a poor relationship with God, done. And we would not have to stand there fearful, because our sins have been forgiven.

A life lived in fear tends not to be fruitful. Not fearing the future frees us to bear fruit now.

## IV. The Fourth Confidence: Access to God in Prayer (1 John 5:14–17)

The fourth and last confidence — the one we will spend more time on — is in the passage we read this morning, verses 14 through 17 of chapter 5.

Christ — and this is important, believe it — Christ has opened the doors of the Most Holy Place wide open. Christ has opened the doors of heaven to you. Do you realize that at any moment, any time you want, you can come before God in prayer, and he will listen to you?

Fathers, have you ever been in the middle of something, with your little kids coming to you — "Dad, dad, dad, dad" — and you say, "I'm busy right now, don't talk to me right now"? God never does that to you. God hears our prayers.

Remember the scene in the Gospels when Jesus says, "It is finished." What happens to the curtain that separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place in the temple? It was torn in two — signifying that access to God is now given to everyone who believes in the Lord Jesus Christ. The Lord Jesus Christ went into the Holy of Holies with his own blood, and with him he brought us all into the presence of God. We can pray with confidence that he will hear our prayers.

The author of Hebrews says in chapter 4 verse 16:

> *"Let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need."* (Hebrews 4:16)

That word *confidence* is the same word for boldness that we have been reading throughout these passages in 1 John.

### The One Limitation: Praying According to His Will

If you read the passage, there is one limitation to God's hearing our prayers. Look again at verse 14:

> *"And this is the confidence that we have toward him, that if we ask anything according to his will he hears us."* (1 John 5:14)

Do you see the limitation? *According to his will.* To pray according to God's will is to pray according to his revealed will — the Bible. We pray prayers that are consistent with what God teaches us in his Word. We do not pray for what is sinful. We pray for what is righteous.

God is not saying that we have to somehow figure out a secret will — something he is privately thinking — and if we can figure out exactly what he is thinking, he will answer. No. The will of God is the Word of God. That is why he calls us to know it, and we pray according to it. We live in light of what he has revealed, not according to what he has not revealed.

In Deuteronomy 29:29, he clearly says: "The secret things belong to the Lord our God, but the things that are revealed belong to us and to our children forever, that we may do all the words of this law." Nowhere in the Bible does God ask you to do something he has not revealed to you in the Word. You do not have to worry: am I in God's will? Are you obeying the Word? Then you are in his will.

### God's Hearing and God's Answering

God's hearing our prayers is not the same as God doing everything exactly the way we ask. Look at verse 15:

> *"And if we know that he hears us in whatever we ask, we know that we have the requests that we have asked of him."* (1 John 5:15)

He says he is going to hear us, and because of that we know he is going to answer. We will have the petitions. But that does not mean he will give us exactly what we ask, word for word. God is aware of all things. He knows what happens today and tomorrow. He knows the consequences of everything. He knows exactly how to answer our prayer so that the most glory will be brought to him. And if you are not satisfied with that, then there is a problem with you, not with God — because God does all things to the praise of his glory.

God answers every single prayer that is brought to him by his people according to his Word, in the best way possible. Putting these two things together — God hears and God answers — this is a great source of encouragement and comfort. Know that every prayer is answered. Even if that answer is the dreaded no. Even if that answer is the dreaded death. Even if that answer is the dreaded end of a relationship. It is a glorious answer according to God's great wisdom for his people.

The author of Hebrews says that when we come to him, we obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need. The very act of praying is itself a mercy, and we find grace in it.

## V. The Exception: Sin That Leads to Death (1 John 5:16–17)

As believers in Christ, we have confidence that we can bring everything before the Son of God, knowing that he will hear our prayers — everything except one thing, he says. Do you notice that? Look at verse 16:

> *"If anyone sees his brother committing a sin not leading to death, he shall ask, and God will give him life — to those who commit sins that do not lead to death. There is sin that leads to death; I do not say that one should pray for that. All wrongdoing is sin, but there is sin that does not lead to death."* (1 John 5:16–17)

God calls us to pray about everything except this one thing: a sin that leads to death.

Who John is talking about is not immediately clear. There are at least two ways to understand what he means by "sin leading to death."

### Interpretation 1: Physical Death

One interpretation is sin that leads to physical death — the person literally dies as a result of their sin. Can you think of examples in the Bible? We have Ananias and Sapphira in Acts chapter 5, who lie to the Holy Spirit and immediately die. We have in 1 Corinthians 11 the people who partake of the Lord's Supper in an unworthy manner — outside of Christ — and they were sick, and some died.

If that is the case, then the person would already be dead. It would be pointless to pray for them. John says: do not pray for that, because they are dead. It is appointed for men once to die and then judgment. After you die, there is no point in praying, because that is it. God is not going to change his mind after we leave this world.

### Interpretation 2: Apostasy — Sin Leading to Eternal Death

The other interpretation is that this refers to sin that leads to eternal death — that is, apostasy: a deliberate denial of the apostolic witness of Christ and his saving power by someone who had professed faith in Christ.

All sin is unto death in one sense — the wages of sin is death; the soul that sins shall die. On the other hand, there is at least this one sin that is unrepentable. John seems to think his original audience will know exactly what he is talking about. I wish he had put some footnotes.

But looking at the context of 1 John — since John has been combating false teachers who once professed belief but now deny Christ, about whom John says "they went out from us, but they were not of us" — this leads us to conclude that the sin unto death is a willful, thought-out, cognitively informed, persistent rejection of Christ: one who has denied Christ after knowing who he is, over and over again, even after being presented with Scripture and the best arguments for who Christ is.

I think what John is saying here corresponds to what the Holy Spirit said through the author of Hebrews, chapter 6 verses 4 through 6:

> *"For it is impossible, in the case of those who have once been enlightened, who have tasted the heavenly gift, and have shared in the Holy Spirit, and have tasted the goodness of the word of God and the powers of the age to come, and then have fallen away, to restore them again to repentance, since they are crucifying once again the Son of God to their own harm and holding him up to contempt."* (Hebrews 6:4–6)

They have rejected the only one who could drive them to repentance. So I think that is what John is talking about in 1 John when he speaks of the sin that leads to death.

### Application: Cling to Christ

If that is what John means by sin unto death — and I believe it is — then what is the message for us?

Brothers and sisters: cling to Christ. Hold on tight to him. That is the means by which you will persevere to the end. Do not let Christ go. Be Jacob who wrestled with God until he received the blessing. Do not buy into any lie concerning Christ. Hold on to him. Cling to Christ by faith.

## VI. Conclusion: Pray for One Another

I want to finish by pointing out that John's emphasis here is not the sin unto death — that is the exception. The emphasis is: pray for people in the church.

After his remarkable statement about our free access to God, John speaks about praying for others. He says: use your access to God to pray for those who are struggling with sin. This passage assumes a community of believers — a church — where people are aware of each other's struggles and pray for one another.

The problem is that we are often too proud to share our struggles with others, and often too uncomfortable when people actually pray for those struggles. That is not what we are called to be as a church. We are called to pray for each other's struggles with sin.

May the Lord grant us grace to be a humble fellowship of believers who share in each other's struggles with sin, as we pray to the only one who can change us. People of God, every one in this room struggles with sin. They may not admit it, but they do. You are not the only one. And as we humble ourselves, as we let people into our lives, and as we pray for one another, we will all grow into the image of Jesus Christ.

We have this great access to the Father. Let us take advantage of it and bring each other before Christ. Do that with confidence in your God. You will be received by Christ at his coming. You will be acquitted at the final judgment. Your sins have been forgiven. Come confidently before your Father in heaven — because he hears you. May he strengthen you in your faith as you confidently follow Jesus.
